title: EP3 — Pictory (3 News Segments)
description: Paste all 3 scripts into Pictory as one video (3 news stories). Render and upload the single MP4.

scripts.label: Story 1: 15% of Americans say they’d be willing to work for an AI boss, according to new poll
scripts.text:
Imagine waking up to a robot's orders - "good morning, report for duty". A new poll suggests that 15% of Americans are open to the idea of working under an AI boss.

The Quinnipiac University survey revealed some surprising results. Among those willing to work for an AI supervisor, nearly half said they'd be comfortable with tasks being assigned and schedules set by a machine. But what does this mean for the future of work?

As automation increasingly takes over routine tasks, it's not hard to imagine a scenario where AI becomes a more prominent presence in the workplace. In fact, some experts predict that within the next decade, many jobs will involve working alongside - or under - artificial intelligence.

But would you really want to report to a robot? Some respondents said yes, citing benefits like increased efficiency and reduced bureaucracy. Others expressed concerns about job security and the loss of human interaction in the workplace.

As AI continues to advance, we're seeing more evidence that humans are willing to adapt to new ways of working. But what does this mean for our sense of purpose and identity at work? Are we prepared to cede control to machines?

While 15% may seem like a small percentage, it's a significant number - especially when you consider the pace of technological change. As AI becomes more sophisticated, it's possible that even more people will be open to the idea of working under machine supervision.

So what comes next? Will we see more companies experimenting with AI-led teams and departments? And how will this shift impact our relationships with colleagues and managers? These are questions worth exploring as we navigate the future of work.

scripts.label: Story 2: Popular AI gateway startup LiteLLM ditches controversial startup Delve
scripts.text:
Breaking news in the world of tech: LiteLLM, a popular AI gateway startup, has made a surprising move just days after being hit with a devastating malware attack.

The company, which had been making waves in the industry with its cutting-edge technology, was compromised by horrific credential-stealing malware last week. This type of attack is a nightmare for any business, as it can leave sensitive data and login credentials vulnerable to hackers.

But what's even more surprising is that LiteLLM obtained two key security compliance certifications through its partnership with Delve, another tech firm. These certifications are like badges of honor in the industry, indicating that a company has met certain standards for security and data protection.

However, it appears that this partnership may have been a liability rather than an asset. Just days after the malware attack, LiteLLM announced that it's parting ways with Delve. The reasons behind this decision are unclear, but it's likely that the company wants to distance itself from any potential security risks associated with its former partner.

This move matters for several reasons. For one, it highlights the importance of carefully choosing partners and vendors in the tech industry. A single misstep can have serious consequences, as LiteLLM has just learned the hard way.

Moreover, this incident serves as a reminder that even the most advanced security measures can be compromised by human error or vulnerabilities in the system. It's a sobering thought for any business that relies on technology to operate securely.

As for what comes next, it's unclear how LiteLLM will recover from this setback. The company has already taken steps to address the malware attack and is working to rebuild trust with its customers. But one thing is certain: the tech industry will be watching closely to see how LiteLLM navigates this challenging situation.

In the meantime, other companies in the AI space are likely taking note of this incident as a warning sign. It's a reminder that even the most cutting-edge technology can't replace good old-fashioned common sense and caution when it comes to security and data protection.

scripts.label: Story 3: As more Americans adopt AI tools, fewer say they can trust the results
scripts.text:
As more Americans turn to AI tools in their daily lives, a growing concern is emerging: can we really trust these digital assistants to have our backs? A new poll from Quinnipiac University has found that as AI adoption rises, so does distrust in its results.

[Visuals of people using AI-powered devices or apps on their phones]

According to the survey, nearly 70% of Americans say they're worried about the accuracy and reliability of AI-generated information. But what's driving this skepticism? Experts point to a lack of transparency and accountability within the tech industry itself. Without clear explanations for how AI decisions are made, it's impossible to know if we can trust the results.

[Visuals of people looking at code or technical diagrams on computer screens]

The issue extends far beyond individual users, too. As AI becomes increasingly integrated into critical systems like healthcare and finance, the stakes are higher than ever. With AI-powered diagnosis tools being used in hospitals, for example, it's crucial that doctors can rely on their accuracy.

[Visuals of a doctor examining patient records on a computer]

But trust is a two-way street – and right now, many Americans feel that tech companies aren't doing enough to build confidence in their products. With calls growing louder for stronger regulations around AI development, one thing's clear: the future of AI depends on our ability to trust its results.

[Visuals of people protesting or holding signs demanding greater transparency]

So what comes next? Policymakers are already grappling with how to address these concerns – from implementing stricter standards for AI development to providing clearer explanations for how these tools work. As we continue to rely more heavily on AI, it's imperative that we prioritize transparency and accountability above all else.

[Visuals of a person looking at a smartphone screen with a puzzled expression]

Ultimately, the question remains: can we trust AI to have our best interests in mind? With so much riding on its accuracy and reliability, one thing's certain – only time will tell.
